---
title: "End of Life geol export database discovery"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    theme:
      dark: darkly
      light: flatly
jupyter: python3
params:
  database: "geol.duckdb"
---

This report is generated from a `geol export` command. For more information about `geol`, visit its GitHub repository: [https://github.com/opt-nc/geol](https://github.com/opt-nc/geol) or explore the [Data Engineering with geol](https://opt-nc.github.io/geol/docs/tutorial-extras/dataengineering/) tutorial for more advanced usage.

```{python}
import duckdb
import os

# Get database parameter, default to 'geol.duckdb'
database = os.getenv('QUARTO_PARAM_DATABASE', 'geol.duckdb')
conn = duckdb.connect(database=database)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
import subprocess
import sys

# List of required packages
required_packages = ['duckdb', 'plotly', 'pandas', 'itables', 'graphviz', 'networkx']

# Check and install missing packages
for package in required_packages:
    try:
        __import__(package)
    except ImportError:
        print(f"Installing {package}...")
        subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-q", package])
```

## Report Information

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from datetime import datetime
import socket
import sys
from IPython.display import HTML, display

# Get generation timestamp
generation_time = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
hostname = socket.gethostname()
db_path = os.path.abspath(database)
db_size = f"{os.path.getsize(db_path) / (1024*1024):.2f} MB" if os.path.exists(db_path) else "N/A"

# Get geol version from about table
try:
    geol_version = conn.execute("SELECT git_version FROM about LIMIT 1").fetchone()[0]
except Exception:
    try:
        geol_version = conn.execute("SELECT version FROM about LIMIT 1").fetchone()[0]
    except Exception:
        geol_version = "Unknown"

report_info_html = f"""
<div style="margin: 20px 0; padding: 20px; border-left: 5px solid #4A90E2; background-color: rgba(74, 144, 226, 0.08); border-radius: 0 10px 10px 0; font-size: 0.95em;">
    <div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr)); gap: 15px;">
        <div><span style="opacity: 0.7;">📅 Generated:</span> <strong>{generation_time}</strong></div>
        <div><span style="opacity: 0.7;">💾 Database:</span> <code style="background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.15); color: inherit; padding: 2px 5px; border-radius: 4px;">{database}</code> <small>({db_size})</small></div>
        <div><span style="opacity: 0.7;">🛠️ Geol Version:</span> <strong>{geol_version}</strong></div>
        <div><span style="opacity: 0.7;">🐍 Engine:</span> <strong>DuckDB {duckdb.__version__} / Python {sys.version.split()[0]}</strong></div>
    </div>
</div>
"""
display(HTML(report_info_html))
```

<div style="text-align: center; margin: 20px 0;">
<a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9AcIMW99-8s" target="_blank" style="display: inline-block; position: relative;">
<img src="https://img.youtube.com/vi/9AcIMW99-8s/maxresdefault.jpg" alt="Geol Introduction Video" style="max-width: 800px; width: 100%; border-radius: 8px; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.1); cursor: pointer; transition: transform 0.2s;" onmouseover="this.style.transform='scale(1.02)'" onmouseout="this.style.transform='scale(1)'"/>
<div style="position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%); font-size: 60px; color: rgba(255,255,255,0.9); text-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.5);">▶️</div>
</a>
<p style="margin-top: 10px; font-size: 0.9em; color: #666;"><em>Click to watch the video on YouTube</em></p>
</div>

# Database Summary

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Get summary stats
from IPython.display import HTML, display

table_names = conn.execute("SELECT table_name FROM duckdb_tables() WHERE schema_name = 'main'").fetchdf()['table_name'].tolist()
table_count = len(table_names)

def get_count(table):
    if table in table_names:
        return conn.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table}').fetchone()[0]
    return None

def format_count(count):
    return f"{count:,}" if count is not None else "N/A"

product_count = format_count(get_count('products'))
category_count = format_count(get_count('categories'))
tag_count = format_count(get_count('tags'))

summary_html = f"""
<div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-around; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 20px; margin: 30px 0;">
    <div style="flex: 1; min-width: 150px; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); text-align: center; border-top: 5px solid #4A90E2;">
        <div style="font-size: 0.9em; opacity: 0.7; text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: bold;">Tables</div>
        <div style="font-size: 2.2em; font-weight: 800;">{table_count}</div>
    </div>
    <div style="flex: 1; min-width: 150px; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); text-align: center; border-top: 5px solid #50C878;">
        <div style="font-size: 0.9em; opacity: 0.7; text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: bold;">Products</div>
        <div style="font-size: 2.2em; font-weight: 800;">{product_count}</div>
    </div>
    <div style="flex: 1; min-width: 150px; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); text-align: center; border-top: 5px solid #FF7F50;">
        <div style="font-size: 0.9em; opacity: 0.7; text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: bold;">Categories</div>
        <div style="font-size: 2.2em; font-weight: 800;">{category_count}</div>
    </div>
    <div style="flex: 1; min-width: 150px; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); background-color: rgba(128, 128, 128, 0.05); text-align: center; border-top: 5px solid #9370DB;">
        <div style="font-size: 0.9em; opacity: 0.7; text-transform: uppercase; font-weight: bold;">Tags</div>
        <div style="font-size: 2.2em; font-weight: 800;">{tag_count}</div>
    </div>
</div>
"""
display(HTML(summary_html))
```

# Exploring the products table

This Quarto document explores the products table of the geol.duckdb database.

## Displaying the first rows of products

```{python}
#| echo: false
from itables import show
import itables.options as opt

# Configure itables for better display
opt.lengthMenu = [5, 10, 25, 50]
opt.maxBytes = 0

products_head = conn.execute("SELECT * FROM products LIMIT 100").fetchdf()
show(products_head, caption="Products Table (Interactive)")
```

## Product distribution by category

```{python}
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import os

# Define consistent chart height for all visualizations
CHART_HEIGHT = 500

# Get the number of products per category
cat_count = conn.execute("""
SELECT c.id AS category, COUNT(p.id) AS num_products
FROM products p
JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id
GROUP BY c.id
ORDER BY num_products DESC
""").fetchdf()

# Create treemap instead of pie chart for better visualization
fig_treemap = px.treemap(cat_count, 
                         path=[px.Constant("All Categories"), 'category'], 
                         values='num_products',
                         title='Product distribution by category (Treemap)',
                         color='num_products',
                         color_continuous_scale='Blues')
fig_treemap.update_traces(textinfo="label+value+percent parent")
fig_treemap
```

## Top 10 categories

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Create a horizontal bar chart for the top 10 categories
top_10_cat = cat_count.head(10)
fig_bar = px.bar(top_10_cat.sort_values(by='num_products', ascending=True), 
                 x='num_products', 
                 y='category', 
                 orientation='h',
                 title='Top 10 categories by number of products',
                 text='num_products',
                 height=CHART_HEIGHT)
fig_bar.update_traces(textposition='outside')
fig_bar.update_layout(yaxis_title='Category', xaxis_title='Number of products')
fig_bar
```

*This chart highlights the top 10 categories by product count. The horizontal layout makes it easy to compare quantities and identify the most populated categories in the catalog.*

## Top 10 tags

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Query to get the count of each tag
tag_count = conn.execute("""
SELECT t.id AS tag, COUNT(pt.product_id) AS num_products
FROM product_tags pt
JOIN tags t ON pt.tag_id = t.id
GROUP BY t.id
ORDER BY num_products DESC
""").fetchdf()

# Create a horizontal bar chart for the top 10 tags
top_10_tags = tag_count.head(10)
fig_tags = px.bar(top_10_tags.sort_values(by='num_products', ascending=True),
                  x='num_products',
                  y='tag',
                  orientation='h',
                  title='Top 10 tags by number of products',
                  text='num_products',
                  height=CHART_HEIGHT)
fig_tags.update_traces(textposition='outside')
fig_tags.update_layout(yaxis_title='Tag', xaxis_title='Number of products')
fig_tags
```

*Tags provide additional metadata for products. This chart shows which tags are most commonly used, indicating popular themes or characteristics across the product catalog.*

## Category-Tag Cross Analysis

This section analyzes the relationships between product categories and tags.

### Heatmap: Category-Tag Distribution

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Get category-tag relationships
cat_tag_matrix = conn.execute("""
SELECT 
    c.id AS category,
    t.id AS tag,
    COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) AS num_products
FROM products p
JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id
JOIN product_tags pt ON p.id = pt.product_id
JOIN tags t ON pt.tag_id = t.id
GROUP BY c.id, t.id
ORDER BY num_products DESC
""").fetchdf()

print(f"Found {len(cat_tag_matrix)} category-tag combinations")

# Pivot for heatmap
pivot_data = cat_tag_matrix.pivot(index='category', columns='tag', values='num_products').fillna(0)

# Create heatmap
fig_heatmap = px.imshow(pivot_data,
                        labels=dict(x="Tag", y="Category", color="Products"),
                        title="Category-Tag Heatmap: Product Distribution",
                        color_continuous_scale='YlOrRd',
                        aspect='auto',
                        height=max(500, len(pivot_data) * 25))
fig_heatmap.update_xaxes(side="bottom")
fig_heatmap
```

*The heatmap reveals patterns in how tags are distributed across categories. Darker cells indicate stronger associations between specific categories and tags, helping identify common tag usage patterns within each category.*

### Sunburst: Hierarchical Category-Tag View

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Create sunburst chart for hierarchical view
cat_tag_sunburst = conn.execute("""
SELECT 
    c.id AS category,
    t.id AS tag,
    COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) AS num_products
FROM products p
JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.id
JOIN product_tags pt ON p.id = pt.product_id
JOIN tags t ON pt.tag_id = t.id
GROUP BY c.id, t.id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) > 0
ORDER BY num_products DESC
LIMIT 100
""").fetchdf()

print(f"Sunburst data: {len(cat_tag_sunburst)} rows")

# Prepare data for sunburst
sunburst_data = []

# Add root
sunburst_data.append({
    'labels': 'All Products',
    'parents': '',
    'values': cat_tag_sunburst['num_products'].sum()
})

# Add categories
cat_totals = cat_tag_sunburst.groupby('category')['num_products'].sum().reset_index()
for _, row in cat_totals.iterrows():
    sunburst_data.append({
        'labels': f"Cat: {row['category']}",
        'parents': 'All Products',
        'values': row['num_products']
    })

# Add tags under categories
for _, row in cat_tag_sunburst.iterrows():
    sunburst_data.append({
        'labels': f"Tag: {row['tag']}",
        'parents': f"Cat: {row['category']}",
        'values': row['num_products']
    })

import pandas as pd
sunburst_df = pd.DataFrame(sunburst_data)

fig_sunburst = go.Figure(go.Sunburst(
    labels=sunburst_df['labels'],
    parents=sunburst_df['parents'],
    values=sunburst_df['values'],
    branchvalues="total",
    marker=dict(colorscale='Blues')
))

fig_sunburst.update_layout(
    title="Category-Tag Hierarchy (Sunburst)",
    height=CHART_HEIGHT
)
fig_sunburst
```

*The sunburst chart provides an interactive hierarchical view from all products down through categories to individual tags. Click on segments to zoom in and explore the nested structure. This visualization helps understand the proportion of tags within each category.*

## Tag Co-occurrence Network

This section analyzes which tags frequently appear together on the same products.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import networkx as nx

# Get tag co-occurrences (tags that appear on the same products)
tag_cooccurrence = conn.execute("""
SELECT 
    t1.id AS tag1,
    t2.id AS tag2,
    COUNT(DISTINCT pt1.product_id) AS co_occurrences
FROM product_tags pt1
JOIN product_tags pt2 ON pt1.product_id = pt2.product_id AND pt1.tag_id < pt2.tag_id
JOIN tags t1 ON pt1.tag_id = t1.id
JOIN tags t2 ON pt2.tag_id = t2.id
GROUP BY t1.id, t2.id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT pt1.product_id) >= 2
ORDER BY co_occurrences DESC
LIMIT 50
""").fetchdf()

print(f"Found {len(tag_cooccurrence)} tag co-occurrence pairs")

# Create network graph
G = nx.Graph()

# Add edges with weights
for _, row in tag_cooccurrence.iterrows():
    G.add_edge(row['tag1'], row['tag2'], weight=row['co_occurrences'])

# Calculate positions using spring layout
pos = nx.spring_layout(G, k=0.5, iterations=50, seed=42)

# Create edge traces
edge_traces = []
for edge in G.edges(data=True):
    x0, y0 = pos[edge[0]]
    x1, y1 = pos[edge[1]]
    weight = edge[2]['weight']
    
    edge_trace = go.Scatter(
        x=[x0, x1, None],
        y=[y0, y1, None],
        mode='lines',
        line=dict(width=weight/2, color='rgba(125,125,125,0.5)'),
        hoverinfo='none',
        showlegend=False
    )
    edge_traces.append(edge_trace)

# Create node trace
node_x = []
node_y = []
node_text = []
node_size = []

for node in G.nodes():
    x, y = pos[node]
    node_x.append(x)
    node_y.append(y)
    
    # Node size based on degree (number of connections)
    degree = G.degree(node)
    node_size.append(10 + degree * 3)
    
    # Hover text
    connections = list(G.neighbors(node))
    node_text.append(f"Tag: {node}<br>Connections: {degree}")

node_trace = go.Scatter(
    x=node_x, y=node_y,
    mode='markers+text',
    text=[node for node in G.nodes()],
    textposition="top center",
    textfont=dict(size=10),
    hoverinfo='text',
    hovertext=node_text,
    marker=dict(
        showscale=True,
        colorscale='Blues',
        size=node_size,
        color=node_size,
        colorbar=dict(
            thickness=15,
            title=dict(text='Connections', side='right'),
            xanchor='left'
        ),
        line=dict(width=2, color='white')
    ),
    showlegend=False
)

# Create figure
fig_network = go.Figure(data=edge_traces + [node_trace],
                       layout=go.Layout(
                           title="Tag Co-occurrence Network",
                           showlegend=False,
                           hovermode='closest',
                           height=CHART_HEIGHT,
                           margin=dict(b=0, l=0, r=0, t=40),
                           xaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False),
                           yaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False)
                       ))
fig_network
```

*The network graph shows relationships between tags that frequently appear together on products. Node size indicates how many connections a tag has (its importance in the network). Line thickness represents the strength of co-occurrence between tag pairs. This helps identify tag clusters and common tagging patterns.*

# Database Schema Overview

This section describes the tables available in the database and their purpose.

## Database Schema Diagram

```{python}
#| echo: false
import graphviz

# Create a new directed graph for the schema
schema = graphviz.Digraph('database_schema', comment='Database Schema')
schema.attr(rankdir='LR', bgcolor='white')
schema.attr('node', shape='plaintext', fontname='Arial', fontsize='10')

# Get all tables
tables_list = conn.execute("""
    SELECT table_name 
    FROM duckdb_tables() 
    WHERE schema_name = 'main'
    ORDER BY table_name
""").fetchdf()

# Build HTML table for each database table
for idx, row in tables_list.iterrows():
    table_name = row['table_name']
    
    # Get columns for this table
    columns_info = conn.execute(f"""
        SELECT column_name, data_type
        FROM duckdb_columns()
        WHERE table_name = '{table_name}' AND schema_name = 'main'
        ORDER BY column_index
    """).fetchdf()
    
    # Build HTML table
    html = f'''<
        <TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="1" CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="4">
        <TR><TD BGCOLOR="#4A90E2" COLSPAN="2"><FONT COLOR="white"><B>{table_name}</B></FONT></TD></TR>
    '''
    
    for _, col in columns_info.iterrows():
        col_name = col['column_name']
        col_type = col['data_type']
        # Highlight primary key (id) and foreign keys (*_id)
        if col_name == 'id':
            html += f'<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT"><B>🔑 {col_name}</B></TD><TD ALIGN="LEFT">{col_type}</TD></TR>'
        elif col_name.endswith('_id'):
            html += f'<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT"><I>{col_name}</I></TD><TD ALIGN="LEFT">{col_type}</TD></TR>'
        else:
            html += f'<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT">{col_name}</TD><TD ALIGN="LEFT">{col_type}</TD></TR>'
    
    html += '</TABLE>>'
    schema.node(table_name, label=html)

# Add relationships based on foreign key naming convention (*_id)
all_tables = tables_list['table_name'].tolist()

for idx, row in tables_list.iterrows():
    table_name = row['table_name']
    
    columns_info = conn.execute(f"""
        SELECT column_name
        FROM duckdb_columns()
        WHERE table_name = '{table_name}' AND schema_name = 'main'
        AND column_name LIKE '%_id' AND column_name != 'id'
    """).fetchdf()
    
    for _, col in columns_info.iterrows():
        col_name = col['column_name']
        # Try to infer referenced table (e.g., category_id -> categories)
        referenced_base = col_name[:-3] # Remove '_id'
        
        # Check for direct match, plural match, or partial match
        potential_targets = [
            t for t in all_tables 
            if t == referenced_base 
            or t == referenced_base + 's' 
            or t == referenced_base + 'es'
            or referenced_base == t[:-1] # reverse check
        ]
        
        if potential_targets:
            # Use the best match (closest in name)
            target = min(potential_targets, key=len)
            schema.edge(target, table_name, label='1:N', color='#666666', fontsize='9', style='dashed')

schema
```

## Tables and their descriptions

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

# Get all tables in the database
tables_info = conn.execute("""
    SELECT table_name, comment 
    FROM duckdb_tables() 
    WHERE schema_name = 'main'
    ORDER BY table_name
""").fetchdf()

# Get column information for each table
for idx, row in tables_info.iterrows():
    table_name = row['table_name']
    comment = row['comment'] if row['comment'] else 'No description available'
    
    print(f"\n### Table: <span style='color: #4A90E2;'>{table_name}</span>\n")
    print(f"**Description:** {comment}\n")
    
    # Get columns for this table
    columns_info = conn.execute(f"""
        SELECT column_name, data_type, comment
        FROM duckdb_columns()
        WHERE table_name = '{table_name}' AND schema_name = 'main'
        ORDER BY column_index
    """).fetchdf()
    
    # Display columns info
    if not columns_info.empty:
        print(f"**Columns ({len(columns_info)}):**\n")
        for _, col in columns_info.iterrows():
            col_comment = f" - {col['comment']}" if col['comment'] else ""
            print(f"- `{col['column_name']}` ({col['data_type']}){col_comment}")
    
    # Get row count
    row_count = conn.execute(f"SELECT COUNT(*) as cnt FROM {table_name}").fetchdf().iloc[0, 0]
    print(f"\n**Row count:** {row_count:,}\n")

# Close database connection
conn.close()
```
